narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted an 88.2% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—a risk level that led to the tough decision to reject your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass that critical hurdle. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school didn't keep pace with peers who typically pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also well under the average around 5.7, added to this concern, reinforcing a pattern of lower rankings compared to successful bar takers. Even your LSAT score of 30, below the dataset average of 36.7, nudged the odds further toward failure, as higher scores are strongly linked to first-time passes. On a brighter note, your upper-middle family income bracket worked slightly against passing in the model's eyes, though less powerfully than your academics. Positively, your undergraduate GPA pushed meaningfully toward a passing prediction, helping offset some risks, and your choice of full-time study aligned with the vast majority of successful students, offering a small boost. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having standout undergrad grades but lagging in school deciles, LSAT, and enough other signals to tip the scales heavily toward predicted failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential—models like this weigh patterns from past data, and your unique story as a Black woman brings resilience that stats can't fully capture. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year academics if possible, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value diverse backgrounds like yours. You have the GPA foundation to build on—keep pushing, and many paths to law remain open. We're rooting for you.
